unresolved.text: If an occult primary exists, this could be a case of metastatic disease, but in a 12-year-old with no prior malignancy, this is unlikely.
The many types of liver masses can be grouped on the basis of underlying cause, such as infection, neoplasm, or inflammation.

The problem with the diagnosis of a primary hepatic malignant tumor is the presentation. Tumors of this type typically present as abdominal masses, often with abdominal discomfort and weight loss; fever is rare, particularly the type of fever that this boy experienced.

The histopathological features, the distribution of the affected arteries, and the absence of antibodies specific to myeloperoxidase or proteinase 3 rule out an ANCA-related vasculitis, but in this case, the clinical and radiological data converge most compellingly on hydatid disease.
The pattern of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.
I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess, but the lack of response to metronidazole and negative serology makes this less likely.

The probability of hydatid disease is high given the epidemiology and imaging findings, and the absence of a clearly defined capsule on MRI is somewhat atypical, but this may be due to rupture, infection, or degeneration.

It has been reported that patients with hydatid disease may have a catastrophic reaction after high-dose corticosteroid therapy is begun, and this patient’s history of swimming in freshwater and exposure to rural environments in multiple endemic countries supports this diagnosis.
